title: Google App Campaigns: Android is a must, iOS requires significant budget
insight: Google's biggest advantage on Android is the Play Store — high-intent inventory you can't buy anywhere else, comparable to branded search. If you have an Android app and aren't running Google App Campaigns, you're doing yourself a disservice. On iOS, Google is less competitive but still has unique inventory (YouTube, Google Search). Minimum recommended spend: $200/day on Android, $500/day on iOS. Some subscription apps need $1,000+/day on iOS for the algorithm to work.
raw_excerpt: At a minimum you're gonna want like 200 bucks a day on Android. On iOS I probably wouldn't invest less than $500 a day... If you have an Android app and you're not running it with Google, like you're doing yourself a disservice.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour / Price Power podcast / SubClub - Pros and Cons of UAC
actionable_steps: Budget minimum $200/day on Android, $500/day on iOS for Google App Campaigns
For subscription apps on iOS, $1,000+/day may be needed for the algorithm to get sufficient signal
Prioritize Android if budget is limited — Play Store inventory is uniquely high-intent
On iOS, consider Google only after saturating Meta and Apple Search Ads

title: Use Firebase SDK on Android for significantly better bidding performance
insight: On Android, bidding on Firebase events (vs Play events or MMP events) produces significantly stronger performance because Google gets much more data through its own SDK. Firebase is a must on Android. On iOS, Firebase doesn't provide the same advantage — take it or leave it. You can start iOS campaigns without an MMP if Google is your only network, but you'll need an MMP if running multiple iOS ad networks to compare via SKAN.
raw_excerpt: If you're bidding on Firebase events as opposed to play events or your MMP events the performance is going to be stronger... Firebase for Android is a must. On iOS take it or leave it.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Implement Firebase SDK and bid on Firebase events for Android campaigns
Link Google Analytics for Firebase to your Google Ads account
On iOS, Firebase is optional — no significant advantage over other event sources
Only need an MMP for iOS if running multiple ad networks

title: Google UAC traffic distribution: 60% video on iOS, 30% on Android due to Play Store
insight: On Android, ~30% of traffic comes from video because the Play Store absorbs a huge share of inventory (text + image ads). On iOS, ~60% of traffic flows through video (mainly YouTube) since Play Store inventory doesn't exist. This means iOS campaigns require much stronger video creative (especially YouTube Shorts-style UGC). On Android, focus more on landscape images and text assets for Play Store placements.
raw_excerpt: On Android about 30% of the traffic comes from videos. On iOS it's closer to 60%... on Android a huge amount of the inventory flows through the Play Store. On iOS the Play Store does not exist and so it has to flow somewhere else and typically it will flow through video.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: For iOS: invest heavily in video creative (60% of traffic), especially short-form UGC for YouTube Shorts
For Android: prioritize landscape images and text assets for Play Store placements
Create videos in multiple durations: 6s, 10s, 20s, 30s, and 30s+ to test across placements
Upload portrait short-form videos to drive more YouTube Shorts traffic

title: Treat text assets like keywords — they index against search terms and control CPMs
insight: Text assets in Google App Campaigns function like keywords because Google indexes them against search queries. Don't just write generic feature descriptions — include specific terms users actually search for (e.g., 'storm tracker' and 'tornado warnings' instead of just 'weather app'). Different keywords in text assets have dramatically different CPMs — text assets mentioning 'AI' had significantly higher CPMs than those without. Use the Google search planner to find high-volume, lower-cost terms.
raw_excerpt: Think about your text assets like keywords because that's how Google indexes them... when I broke it out and aggregated all of the text assets that had the mention AI in it versus those that didn't you see a huge difference in the CPM.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Write text assets using specific long-tail search terms your users would type
Focus on pain points, not just features, in text asset copy
Analyze CPM by text asset — some keywords are dramatically more expensive than others
Use Google's search planner to discover high-volume, lower-cost terms
Add new ad groups with different keyword-oriented text assets to scale without raising bids

title: Google UAC serves across 5 networks — understand what's really behind each traffic source
insight: Google App Campaigns serve across 5 networks but the dashboard only shows 4 buckets. 'Search' includes both google.com AND Play Store search. 'Display Network' includes AdMob (in-app ads), MGDN (mobile browser ads), AND Play Store browse. 'YouTube' includes Shorts, in-stream, homepage, and bumper ads. You need a Google rep (typically $30K+/month spend) to see the granular breakout. Use Network segmentation in Google Ads to monitor traffic shifts across these buckets.
raw_excerpt: Google search actually encompasses both google.com searches but it also includes play search... the Display Network includes admob, MGDN which is mobile Google Display Network, and it also includes play inventory, play browse... in the front end this is all you can see.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour / SubClub - Pros and Cons of UAC
actionable_steps: Use the 'Segment by Network' view in Google Ads to monitor traffic distribution
Watch for sudden shifts in display vs search vs YouTube — they can signal performance problems
If you see a performance drop, check if traffic shifted to low-quality display/web placements
Use the app placement report to identify and block underperforming apps/sites
Need $30K+/month spend to get a Google rep who can show granular placement breakouts

title: Never change Google UAC bids or budgets more than 20% per day
insight: Google's algorithm is extremely sensitive to bid and budget changes. Never adjust more than 20% in a single day on any campaign — larger changes frequently cause campaigns to tank and struggle to recover. This makes scaling slow compared to Meta. If you need to scale significantly, add new ad groups with different text-asset strategies to open new inventory pockets rather than aggressively increasing bids on existing campaigns.
raw_excerpt: Google will tell you if you're going to make any changes to your bids or budgets please don't do it more than 20% in a given day. Believe them. I've seen it too many times where people just cause your campaign to tank and it has a hard time recovering.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Never change bids or budgets more than 20% per day on any single campaign
Scale gradually — this means slow ramp times compared to Meta
To scale faster, add new ad groups with different text asset strategies
Add English-language assets in non-English markets to unlock additional inventory
If a campaign stalls, try duplicating it fresh rather than aggressively tweaking

title: Duplicate stale Google UAC campaigns for a fresh start — algorithms get fatigued
insight: Google App Campaign algorithms can burn out over time — scale degrades even without changes. When you notice a campaign that was performing well starts declining and can't sustain volume, try duplicating the campaign and launching the copy. The fresh campaign often finds new audiences and recovers performance. This is a common insider technique for reviving stalled campaigns.
raw_excerpt: Sometimes the algorithm just needs a fresh start. They can burn out at times, get fatigued. If I notice the scale was good but then for whatever reason it can't sustain anymore, go ahead and replicate it and launch it again.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: If a campaign's performance degrades over weeks/months, duplicate it and launch fresh
Don't keep tweaking a dying campaign — sometimes a clean start works better
Be patient with new campaigns — Google's learning phase takes longer than Meta's

title: Ignore Google's built-in creative performance rating — it measures scalability, not value
insight: Google's asset performance rating in the Ads dashboard (low/good/best) is based on an asset's ability to scale and drive high click-through rates — not on whether it drives valuable conversions for your business. An asset rated 'low' can actually have the cheapest CPI. Seasoned Google Ads practitioners universally agree this rating is useless. Evaluate creative performance by your own metrics (CPA, ROAS) instead.
raw_excerpt: Google has a performance rating built into the system. You can just throw it out the window. The rating is based on assets that have the ability to scale and have high click-through rates. This particular asset has a low rating but it's actually the cheapest CPI asset.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Ignore Google's built-in asset performance ratings (low/good/best)
Evaluate creative performance by your own CPA, ROAS, or down-funnel metrics
Don't remove 'low-rated' assets — they may be your most cost-effective performers

title: Google doesn't auto-exclude existing users — manually set up audience exclusions
insight: Google App Campaigns do not automatically stop showing ads to people who already have your app. You must manually create exclusion lists for existing users, purchasers, and optionally reinstallers. Also consider excluding your brand terms to prevent paying for organic installs — though removing brand terms caused a 60% install decline for one client with only a modest organic increase, so test carefully. Keyword exclusions are now available in the UI (buried in admin settings).
raw_excerpt: Google doesn't automatically stop your ads from serving to people who already have your app. So you have to enter it manually... I did it for one customer and they saw a 60% decline in installs but didn't see their organics increase by 60%.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour / Price Power podcast
actionable_steps: Create audience exclusion lists for existing users and recent purchasers
Decide whether to exclude reinstallers based on whether those users have value
Test excluding brand keywords carefully — measure organic uplift vs install decline
Find keyword exclusions in the admin section of Google Ads UI

title: Target ROAS campaigns: start 20%+ below your actual target and use 7-day windows
insight: For Target ROAS (tROAS) campaigns on Google, set your initial ROAS bid 20% or more below your actual target to give the algorithm room to find users at scale. Sometimes you need to go even lower. Use a 7-day conversion window max — 30 or 90 days is too long for Google to optimize effectively. Do the backend math to figure out what 7-day ROAS you need to hit your long-term target. tROAS bids work inversely to CPA bids: raising tROAS restricts scale (fewer qualifying users), lowering it expands scale.
raw_excerpt: Start with a shorter window, 7 days for example... if you know you want a 50% ROAS by day 7, then you should go in 20% lower, starting at 40%. In my experience I've had to go even lower to get volume... it's almost the opposite with a ROAS bid — if you bump it up you're putting more strain on Google.
source_title: Price Power podcast - Google App Campaigns, Value-Based Bidding, and Signal Optimization
actionable_steps: Set initial tROAS bid 20%+ below your actual target
Use a 7-day conversion window maximum — longer windows hurt optimization
Back-calculate what 7-day ROAS corresponds to your long-term target (e.g., day-90 ROAS)
Gradually increase the ROAS target once volume stabilizes
Expect a 2-6 week learning period depending on how aggressive your target is

title: tROAS requires Firebase, high event volume, and risk tolerance — not for everyone
insight: Target ROAS campaigns on Google require: (1) Firebase SDK (mandatory for tROAS), (2) high volume of conversion events — minimum 10 revenue events/day (Google's guideline), ideally much more, (3) short trial periods (7-day trials work, 30-day trials are problematic because $30K+ could be spent before the first signal returns), and (4) risk tolerance for a steep 2-6 week learning window. If you only generate 5 trial starts/day with 50% conversion, you may need 20+ trials/day minimum. Currently Android-focused; iOS tROAS is too new to recommend.
raw_excerpt: If you're only generating like five start trials per day, that's just not enough data... you're probably going to need at a minimum 20 start trials so that we're getting enough data back on those revenue conversions... you have to be doing this on Firebase in order to use the TROAZ product.
source_title: Price Power podcast - Google App Campaigns, Value-Based Bidding, and Signal Optimization
actionable_steps: Ensure Firebase SDK is implemented (required for tROAS)
Need 10+ revenue events/day minimum, ideally 20+ trial starts if 50% convert to paid
Avoid tROAS if trial period is 30+ days — signal returns too slowly
Budget for 2-6 weeks of learning with potentially negative ROAS
Consider running CPA campaign alongside tROAS to maintain scale

title: Run CPI/CPA and tROAS campaigns side by side — tROAS alone struggles to scale
insight: tROAS campaigns tend to have difficulty scaling because they target a finite pool of high-value users. Run a CPI or CPA campaign alongside tROAS to maintain volume while the tROAS campaign delivers higher quality. In a geo-split test, the CPA campaign spent significantly more than tROAS but tROAS performance was better on profitability. Avoid running two identical campaign types (e.g., two CPA campaigns) in the same geo — they'll cannibalize each other. Different bid types accessing different inventory generally don't compete.
raw_excerpt: ROAS campaigns have a hard time scaling... what I do see some folks doing is running a CPI or CPA campaign in conjunction with a ROAS campaign to maintain the scale but then also deliver the quality... we did a geo-split test, the CPA campaign definitely spent a lot more versus the TROAZ campaign.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour / Price Power podcast
actionable_steps: Run CPA and tROAS campaigns in parallel — tROAS for quality, CPA for volume
Use geo-split tests to compare CPA vs tROAS performance cleanly
Don't run two identical bid-type campaigns in the same geo — they'll cannibalize
Different bid types (CPI, CPA, tROAS) access different inventory and generally don't compete

title: Optimize for deeper funnel events that predict retention, not just trial starts
insight: If trial-start optimization yields users who don't stick around, find a deeper in-app event that correlates with retention and has sufficient volume (happens within 24 hours, 10+ events/day). For a messaging app, optimizing for 'started a conversation' showed much better retention than 'registration complete'. For an ad-funded utility app, bidding on day-1 retention events improved quality enough to justify higher CPAs. Always think: what action in the first 24 hours predicts long-term value?
raw_excerpt: What we ended up finding is that it's not so much the registration complete but if they actually start a conversation with somebody, that is when we see the stickiness improve... we need something that happened within 24 hours, really quick, so we could still send that signal back to Google.
source_title: Price Power podcast - Google App Campaigns, Value-Based Bidding, and Signal Optimization
actionable_steps: Identify in-app events within 24 hours that correlate with long-term retention/conversion
Event must have sufficient volume (10+/day) to be usable as optimization target
Consider events just after trial start: tutorial completion, first core action, first content consumed
Switch to the deeper event if trial-start optimization yields low-quality users
Do correlation analysis between early events and D7/D30 retention or trial-to-paid conversion

title: Google iOS campaigns: mostly modeled conversions with ~40%+ discrepancy from MMP data
insight: On iOS, Google models (estimates) most conversions rather than deterministically tracking them. This creates huge discrepancies — Google's interface may report 1,000 purchases while your MMP shows 10. Google doesn't ingest SKAN data to optimize campaigns; it only uses SKAN installs to validate whether its own modeling was accurate. Don't optimize Google campaigns based on SKAN data — it doesn't feed back into the system. Google's iOS product is improving: YouTube started prompting for ATT ~late 2024, and IP-based modeling improvements are expected.
raw_excerpt: On iOS totally different ball game. Google shows you what they believe happened... you tend to see huge discrepancies... Google said the only thing they do with SKAN data is use the installs to basically validate was our modeling accurate but they're not ingesting that data to optimize campaigns.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour / Price Power podcast
actionable_steps: Don't trust Google's iOS conversion numbers at face value — they're heavily modeled
Cross-reference with MMP data and product analytics to assess true iOS performance
Don't make Google campaign optimizations based on SKAN data — it's not used by the algorithm
Google's iOS improvements are ongoing — revisit periodically as IP-based modeling rolls out

title: Don't copy Meta creative strategy to Google — different placements need different creative
insight: Google UAC serves across fundamentally different placement types than Meta (Play Store, Google Search, AdMob, mobile browsers, YouTube variants). Creative that works on Instagram Reels may not work on YouTube in-stream or Play Store ad units. You can influence placement distribution through creative format: uploading only portrait short-form video will push traffic to YouTube Shorts; landscape images get more Play Store inventory on Android. Design creative specifically for each Google placement type.
raw_excerpt: Please stop comparing Google with other platforms. I see so many people just copy and paste their meta strategy over to Google. They're just different... your experience on Instagram might be different than your experience on YouTube or in another app.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Create Google-specific creative — don't just reuse Meta ads
Upload portrait short-form video to push traffic toward YouTube Shorts
Use landscape images on Android to capture Play Store inventory
Create videos in multiple durations (6s, 10s, 20s, 30s+) for different YouTube placements
Android: stock images from Google Ads library work just as well as custom images

title: Your app icon matters in Google UAC — it becomes the ad on Play Store placements
insight: Google scrapes your app store listing to build ads, and many Play Store ad units consist of just your app name, icon, and an install button. If your icon doesn't clearly communicate what your app does, you're losing conversions on one of the highest-volume inventory sources. A card game app with a dog character holding cards is immediately recognizable; an abstract icon tells users nothing. Test different app icons as part of your UAC optimization.
raw_excerpt: A lot of times an ad will literally just be the name of the app, the icon and an install now button... the dog with the cards, you probably know what that's going to be. The one on the right I have absolutely no idea.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Ensure your app icon clearly communicates what your app does
Test different app icons — they directly impact UAC performance on Play Store placements
Remember: many Play Store ad units are just icon + name + install button

title: Google's algorithm optimizes for Google first — don't trust it blindly
insight: Google positions UAC as fully automated, but the algorithm still prioritizes Google's revenue alongside advertiser performance. If a campaign isn't hitting your CPA target, Google won't automatically stop spending your money. You still need to actively monitor, optimize, and make tweaks. Google's limited transparency (blended placement data, modeled conversions) makes it harder to verify performance claims. Don't assume automation means hands-off — treat it as a tool that requires ongoing management.
raw_excerpt: Google's still a business... if you looked at the algorithm lineup it would be like hey Google first, get customers, good performance... you can't trust that the system's going to always be doing exactly what you think it should be doing. It still requires you to go in, make optimizations.
source_title: Price Power podcast - Google App Campaigns, Value-Based Bidding, and Signal Optimization
actionable_steps: Actively monitor campaigns — don't assume automation handles everything
Use Network segmentation to detect traffic shifts to low-quality inventory
Cross-reference Google's reported metrics with your own analytics
Use the placement report to identify and block underperforming apps/sites

title: Google UAC has 5 traffic sources with very different characteristics and quality
insight: Google App Campaigns serve across 5 distinct traffic sources: (1) Play Store search/browse — highest intent, like branded search, Android only; (2) Google.com search — high intent, browser-based; (3) YouTube — multiple sub-placements (Shorts, in-stream, homepage, bumpers); (4) AdMob — ads inside other apps, quality varies by vertical (great for games, mixed for utilities); (5) MGDN — mobile browser display ads. A lot of Google's mobile traffic is browser-based (search, MGDN), unlike Meta/TikTok which are app-native. This is why SKAN had challenges capturing Google conversions — web-to-app wasn't counted until SKAN 4.0.
raw_excerpt: On Google we're talking about 5 primary sources of traffic... a lot more of Google's mobile traffic is served in browser in comparison to app native platforms like Meta or TikTok... SKAN wasn't even counting web to app conversions.
source_title: SubClub - The Pros and Cons of Google App Campaigns (UAC)

title: Google recommends 50 installs/day or 10 actions/day — aim for double those numbers
insight: Google's official recommendation is 50 installs/day for CPI campaigns and 10 post-install actions/day (e.g., trial starts) for CPA campaigns. However, these minimums are too low in practice — aim for 100 installs/day or 30 actions/day for the algorithm to optimize effectively. More data always helps the algorithm learn faster and make better decisions. This is why small budgets struggle on Google.
raw_excerpt: Google will give you advice that you need a certain number of events... 50 installs per day and then as low as 10 actions per day... I would always say you need more than whatever the benchmark is. If they're telling you 50, aim for 100. If it's 10 actions per day I would aim for 30.
source_title: SubClub - 10 years of Google app campaigns insight in 1 hour
actionable_steps: Aim for 100+ installs/day for CPI campaigns (Google says 50)
Aim for 30+ actions/day for CPA campaigns (Google says 10)
If you can't hit these volumes, consolidate to fewer campaigns to concentrate signal

title: tROAS campaigns tend to have higher CPMs but deliver better quality traffic
insight: When switching from CPA to tROAS bidding on Google, expect CPMs to increase. Higher CPMs in this context signal higher-quality traffic — Google is bidding more aggressively for users it predicts will generate revenue. Don't chase the cheapest CPM or CPI; low CPMs often mean low-quality users who install but never convert. The tROAS campaign may show lower volume but better profitability than CPA campaigns.
raw_excerpt: I tend to find that the CPM is a lot higher on ROAS campaigns than on CPA campaigns. Again, we're talking usually with the CPM going up, you're talking about higher quality traffic... we don't really just want the cheapest install.
source_title: Price Power podcast - Google App Campaigns, Value-Based Bidding, and Signal Optimization
actionable_steps: Expect higher CPMs on tROAS campaigns — this is a signal of quality, not waste
Don't optimize for the cheapest CPI — cheap installs often mean non-converting users
Monitor placement distribution on tROAS campaigns — traffic may shift to search-heavy inventory
